coredata.dc:title: Efficient and stable hydrogen evolution based on earth-abundant SnSe nanocrystals
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Haiguang
Wang, Chao
Liu, Guiju
Barba, David
Vidal, François
Han, Guangting
Rosei, Federico
coredata.dc:description: Tin chalcogenide nanocrystals (NCs) exhibit a wide absorption spectrum with excellent optoelectronic properties. Both Sn and Se are relatively less harmful and less expensive compared to heavy-metals and precious elements. However, it is still a challenge to directly synthesize SnSe NCs without using toxic and sensitive precursors. Here, we use a cation exchange assisted approach to synthesize SnSe NCs that are optically active in the near infrared (NIR). The SnSe NCs have a cuboid shape obtained thorough an orientated growth path. A typical indirect bandgap was found in these NCs ranging from 0.9 to 1.2 eV. As a proof-of-concept, the SnSe NCs were used as light absorbers for photoelectrochemical (PEC) hydrogen evolution, leading to a saturated photocurrent density of 8.2 mA/cm2, a record value for PEC devices using heavy-metal-free NIR NCs. Our results indicate that SnSe NCs of cuboid shape have great potential for use in emerging optoelectronic and photovoltaic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cation exchange
Earth abundant
SnSe nanocrystals
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and structure of SnSe NCs
Size dependent bandgap in SnSe NCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: H.G. Zhao acknowledges funding from the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2018MB001 ). F.R. acknowledges funding from the Natural Science and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) , the Canada Foundation for Innovation (CFI) for infrastructure support and its operating funds and the Fonds de recherche du Québec – Nature et technologies (FRQNT) . F.R. thanks the Canada Research Chairs program for funding and partial salary support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of SnSe NCs synthesized at different temperatures. (a–c) Reaction temperature at 200 °C. (d–f) Atomic model for NCs viewed along different directions. (g–i) Reaction temperature at 160 °C, 120 °C, and 100 °C, respectively. The insets of Fig. 1(b) and (c) are the fast Fourier transforms of the TEM micrographs, and those of Fig. 1(g–i) are HRTEM images.
Scheme of the NC growth process and reaction mechanism. Cation exchange at early reaction stage, followed by oriented growth.

(a) Absorption spectra of CdSe QDs before and after cation exchange after 10-min reaction at different temperature. (b) Tauc plot used to estimate the indirect band gaps of NCs (100 °C). (c) Measured bandgap energy (red hollow triangler whith error bars) and bandgap energies calculated using Eq. (2) for the NC sizes given in Table 1 (black dots). Case 1–5 correspond to temperatures from 100 °C to 200 °C in the same order. The three sets of black dots for each case result from the uncertainties on the NC sizes. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

J-V (versus RHE) of NCs/TiO2-sensitized photoelectrodes in the dark (black curve), under continuous (red curve) and chopped (green curve) illumination (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW/cm2). The SnSe NCs were synthesized at (a) 200 °C; (b) 160 °C; (c) 120 °C and (d) 100 °C. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic diagram and approximate band alignment of SnSe-sensitized PEC device. (b) Calculated electron and hole energy levels relative to the lowest energy level of the conduction band for the NC sizes reported in Table 1. (c) Normalized photocurrent density versus illumination time at 0.6 V versus RHE under AM 1.5 G illumination (100 mW cm−2) for the optimized photoanodes based on SnSe NCs synthesized at 100 °C for 10 min).
Size/shape parameters, bandgap and saturated J of PEC devices based on SnSe NCs synthesized at different temperature.

head.ce:title: Efficient and stable hydrogen evolution based on earth-abundant SnSe nanocrystals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles & College of Physics, Qingdao University, No. 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles & College of Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles & College of Physics, Qingdao University, No. 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Energy, Materials and Telecommunications, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique, 1650 Boulevard Lionel-Boulet Varennes, Québec J3X 1S2, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Energy, Materials and Telecommunications, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique, 1650 Boulevard Lionel-Boulet Varennes, Québec J3X 1S2, Canada

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tin chalcogenide nanocrystals (NCs) exhibit a wide absorption spectrum with excellent optoelectronic properties. Both Sn and Se are relatively less harmful and less expensive compared to heavy-metals and precious elements. However, it is still a challenge to directly synthesize SnSe NCs without using toxic and sensitive precursors. Here, we use a cation exchange assisted approach to synthesize SnSe NCs that are optically active in the near infrared (NIR). The SnSe NCs have a cuboid shape obtained thorough an orientated growth path. A typical indirect bandgap was found in these NCs ranging from 0.9 to 1.2 eV. As a proof-of-concept, the SnSe NCs were used as light absorbers for photoelectrochemical (PEC) hydrogen evolution, leading to a saturated photocurrent density of 8.2 mA/cm2, a record value for PEC devices using heavy-metal-free NIR NCs. Our results indicate that SnSe NCs of cuboid shape have great potential for use in emerging optoelectronic and photovoltaic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor nanocrystals (NCs) have been widely studied due to their potential use in future optoelectronic technologies [1,2]. They are widely used as building blocks in photonic devices, such as solar cells, luminescent solar concentrators, solar-driven water splitting, light emitting devices, thermoelectrics [1–10]. To date, most high-quality colloidal semiconductor NCs (e.g. PbS, PbS/CdS, CdSe, CdSe/ZnS) contains heavy-toxic-metals of Pb or Cd [11–20]. For example, as PbS quantum dots (QDs) have a narrow bandgap, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) devices based on PbS QDs exhibit a saturated photocurrent density (J) as high as 22.1 mA/cm2, which is a record value for hydrogen (H2) evolution among various types of colloidal semiconductor NCs [21]. The core/multiple shell CdSe/CdSSe/CdS QDs based PEC devices have a J reaching up to 17.5 mA/cm [2,22]. However, it is still very challenging to find a high-quality, heavy-metal-free NC system to replace these heavy-metals [23]. Although group III−V semiconductor NCs (e.g. InAs, InSb), binary and ternary metal chalcogenides [e.g. CuInSe, AgInSe2, Ag2Se, Cu(In,Ga)Se2)] have narrow bandgaps, In and Ag are not abundant elements, so that their integration into devices increases production costs [1,6,23–26]. As a result, the use of heavy-metals limits their use in commercial technologies. Current research directions for solar-driven hydrogen evolution privilege the implementation of earth-abundant elements, which are also less toxic and cost-effective materials [23].

Tin chalcogenides (SnX; X = S, Se, and Te) are important IV–VI semiconductors that are being widely studied [3,10,26–39] because (i) both Sn and Se (or S, Te) are less toxic compared to Cd or Pb based materials; (ii) Sn and Se (or S, Te) are more earth abundant and relatively environmentally-friendly elements; (iii) bulk SnSe (or SnS and SnTe) is considered as a promising alternative for narrow band gap materials. For example, bulk SnSe has an indirect band gap of 0.90 eV and a direct band gap of 1.30 eV, and bulk SnTe has a direct band gap of 0.18 eV at 300 K, which promote the optical absorption from the UV–vis to the infrared regions of the solar spectrum [28,30]. These properties make tin chalcogenide NCs suitable as excellent candidates for near-infrared optoelectronics (e.g. solar cells, solar-driven hydrogen evolution), photodetectors and thermoelectric devices [10,26,34,37,38].

To date, SnSe NCs with various sizes/shapes have been produced via wet-chemical approaches [10,30,34,36,38]. However, the SnSe NCs were synthesized using highly reactive and unstable Sn precursors Sn[N(SiMe3)2]2 or highly environmentally sensitive and unstable Se precursors (Selenourea) [30,36,40]. There are very few reports on the synthesis of SnSe NCs with less reactive and relative stable precursors, such as oleylamine-SnCl2 and oleic acid-Se, while the reaction temperature needs to be higher than 146 °C for the growth of NCs and the temperature for Se precursor preparation is over 300 °C [10,30]. These fabrication parameters generate technical constraints that necessitate the use of a glove box and limit the large-scale and low-cost production of these SnSe NCs. To overcome these issues, cation exchange reactions have recently been extensively investigated [33,41]. During this process, only one type of precursor is used to complete the reaction. As cation exchange reactions are kinetically controlled, this method can be employed to produce at great variety of semiconductors (e.g. Pb2+→ Cd+; Cu+→ Cd2+; In3+ → Cu+; Sn2+ ↔ Cd2+) [33,41,42]. To the best of our knowledge, there is still no report for producing SnSe NCs with tunable size/shape via a cation exchange assisted reaction.

Here we synthesized SnSe NCs with size/shape-dependent quantum confinement via a cation exchange assisted reaction. The as-synthesized SnSe NCs are found to have an orthorhombic crystal structure (from 9 ± 1 nm to 23 ± 5 nm). We further investigated the influence of the reaction parameters on their shape, crystal structure and band gap of the formed NCs. The SnSe NCs exhibit a strong size/shape dependent bandgap, consistent with simulations. We then used the SnSe NCs with different size/shape as photosensitizers for PEC hydrogen evolution. A saturated J as high as ∼8.2 mA/cm2 was obtained for specific NC sizes and shapes. The PEC device has a stable current density of 5.2 mA/cm2 from 2−5 h upon illumination (100 mW/cm2). Our results evidently demonstrate that SnSe NCs are promising for efficient and stable hydrogen evolution and other advanced optoelectronics.

Tin bromide (SnBr2, reagent grade 98 %), oleylamine (technical grade 70 %), oleic acid (technical grade 90 %), selenium powder (Se, 99.99 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Zinc acetate, sodium sulﬁde (Na2S), sodium sulﬁte (Na2SO3), hexadecyltrimethylammoniumbromide (CTAB), toluene, methanol, and ethanol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as-received.

CdSe QDs were synthesized using a previously reported hot injection approach [22]. Details were included in the supporting information (S.I.). The SnSe NCs were synthezied using a cation exchange approach. Typically, SnBr2 (100 mg) was mixed with 10 mL of oleylamine. The mixture was degassed at 40 °C for 5 min and gradually increases to a certain temperature (100−200 °C). One milliliter of the CdSe QDs suspension (4 × 10−7 mol in hexane) was added swiftly into the above mixture and the reacton time was conducted for 30 s or 10 min. The injection and growth temperature for SnSe NCs are kept identical. Subsequently, the reaction temperatue was natually decreased to 40 °C and oleic acid (5 mL) was added. After 20 min stirring, ethanol was added, then the suspension was centrifuged and the supernatant was removed. The NCs were then dispersed in toluene for further characterizations.

A drop-casting approach was used to prepare TiO2 mesoporous film as we reported previously [22]. Details are included in the S.I. The NCs (one batch) were purified twice and dispersed in 20 mL toluene before electrophoretic deposition (EPD, 200 V for 2 h). The anode was further diped in CTAB methanol solution (10 mg/mL) for 60 s. Subsequently, the photoanode was kept at 150 °C for 10 min under vacuum conditions. Finally, two cycles of ZnS were coated on the photoanode using a successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) [5,22].

X-ray diffraction (XRD)analysis was performed on a Philips X’pert diffractometer using a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15418 nm). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEOL 2100F TEM equipped with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was performed in a VG Escalab 220i-XL equipped with a hemispherical analyzer. Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) was used to measure the elements of Cd, Se and Sn. The absorption spectra were acquired by using a Cary 5000 UV–visible–near infrared spectrophotometer (Varian). The fluorescence spectra of the CdSe QDs and SnSe NCs (synthesized at 100 °C) were obtained via a Fluorolog-3 system (Horiba JobinYvon).

The performance of the SnSe/TiO2 anode was measured by using a three-electrode configuration. All potentials were measured with respect to Ag/AgCl during the PEC test using a Metrohm Autolab (PGSTAT302N) and were converted to the RHE scale with the following expression VRHE = VAg/AgCl + 0.197 + pH × 0.059 [5]. The photoresponse was measured using an Oriel LCS-100 solar simulator (AM1.5G, 100 mW/cm2). The working area of the photoanode was ∼0.15 cm2. Hydrogen evolution was measured during the PEC experiment. The produced H2 gas was detected using a Shimadzu GC-8A gas chromatographer (GC) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector. Argon was used as carrier gas for GC analysis. Detailed information about the calculation of the hydrogen generation rate is included in the S.I.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structure of SnSe NCs
ce:section.ce:para: CdSe QDs with core size of 2.7 nm were produced through a hot injection approach [22]. Subsequently a cation exchange method was used to produce SnSe NCs using as-synthesized CdSe QDs as template [43]. After injection of QDs into pre-heated SnBr2-oleylaime at different temperatures, the colour of the solution changes rapidly. No significant change in color was observed when the reaction temperature is lower than 100 °C, indicating that no cation exchange reaction occurs. To compare the effect of the reaction temperatures on the morphology of SnSe NCs, identical SnBr2-oleylaime precursor concentration, CdSe QDs concentration, reaction time and QDs injection rate are used for sample preparation.

SAED patterns and XRD (Figs. S1 and S2) of as-synthesized SnSe NCs show the signature of orthorhombic crystal structures (JCPDS, No. 00-032-1382) with lattice parameters of a = 11.42 Å, b = 4.19 Å, c = 4.46 Å, α = β = γ = 90°. No other diffraction peaks are observed, indicating a high degree of purity. The Cd ions in the CdSe seeds are successfully replaced by Sn ions as evidenced by the appearance of Sn and Se peak in XPS and EDS, (Figs. S3 and S4). This conclusion was further confirmed by ICP-OES measurements, in which only Sn and Se were observed, without the presence of Cd. Fig. 1 (a) and (g–i) are TEM images of SnSe NCs synthesized at different temperatures (100−200 °C), showing the NCs have square and rectangle two-dimensional (2D) shapes. The number of NCs having square 2D outlines is more important than that with rectangle 2D shapes, indicating that the three-dimensional (3D) shape of the NCs is cuboid.

To clearly understand the reaction mechanism, we synthesized NCs at different reaction temperatures and reaction times, while keeping other reaction parameters identical. At a very short reaction time (30 s), double size distribution was observed for SnSe NCs synthesized at different temperatures (Figs. S7 and S8). ICP, XRD and HRTEM measurements prove that at this stage of the reaction, all the CdSe QDs were cation exchanged to SnSe NCs. As shown in Fig. S7, at 180 °C, the size of the NCs that form for a reaction time of 30 s are distributed around two different values: a large one of 10−20 nm and a small one of 2.1 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. S9), which is smaller than that the CdSe QDs seed (2.8 ± 0.5 nm) (Fig. S10). Similar ‘double-size’ distributions were found for other reaction temperatures (160, 180 °C, Fig. S8). For longer reaction times, only large NCs of 10-20 nm diameters are observed in the studied samples (100−200 °C). We infer that cation exchange occurs at the early stage of the reaction (first 30 s), as evidenced by the presence of small SnSe NCs in Fig. S7, which are found to be smaller than that of the CdSe seeds observed on Figs. S7–S9. As all the SnSe NCs maintain single crystalline features (Fig. 1), no oriented attachment or coalescence reaction happened during crystal growth. Otherwise, a large number of SnSe NCs containing crystal boundaries or defects would be observed by HRTEM [44–46], which is not observed in Fig. 1. Based on the TEM images recorded for different reaction conditions, this suggests that the cation exchange process is a two-step mechanism: starting with (i) a fast cation exchange reaction that is evidenced by the fast color change after injecting the CdSe QDs, and followed by (ii) an oriented crystal growth process (Fig. 2 ). Due to the size-distribution of SnSe NCs and their very small diameters, Ostwald ripening may govern the whole reaction. The smaller NCs gradually dissolve and release the Sn and Se monomer to feed the formation of larger NCs. After 10 min reaction, the NCs exhibit a uniform size- distribution (Fig. S6, Table 1). For similar reaction times, higher temperature leads to a faster diffusion rate of monomers (or faster Ostwald ripening), leading to the faster growth of the NCs. The growth rate along [011] direction is faster than [100] direction. Therefore, the cubic NCs transform into cuboid gradually with the growth of crystal. Higher quality 2D-like NCs can be produced at higher temperature (Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size dependent bandgap in SnSe NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the narrow bandgap of bulk SnSe materials, SnSe NCs exhibit a broad absorption spectrum ranging from 300 to 900 nm, with significant overlap with the Sun’s emission spectrum [30,47]. Unlike the presence of a first-excitonic absorption peak around 500 nm in CdSe QDs (Fig. 3 a), there is no obvious excitonic absorption reported for SnSe NCs, which is a typical behaviour in SnSe NCs [30,34,47]. Using the tauc plot [(αhν)0.5 vs. hν] [48], based on the absorption spectrum of the NCs for indirect-allowed optical transitions, we calculated their bandgaps (Fig. 3b). As shown in Table 1, the bandgaps of NCs are found to strongly depend of both their size and shape. The large NCs having H/L ratios of 13 nm/23 nm exhibit a small bandgap of 0.93 eV, which is close to the bulk value of indirect SnSe bandgap (0.9 eV). With the decrease of H (or L), the bandgap increases from 0.93 eV to 1.18 eV (H/L: 7 nm/9 nm) (Table 1), indicating the occurrence of stronger quantum confinement effects in small SnSe NCs. Actually the largest bandgap of 1.18 eV is still quite lower than the minimum direct bandgap value of the bulk SnSe material (1.3 eV). These results suggest that the as-synthesized SnSe NCs have an indirect bandgap, other than direct bangap. The calculated bandgap of the NCs (H/L: 7 nm/9 nm) is consistent with the bandgap value calculated based on its emission peak position (1.3 eV) (Fig. S11), further indicating the indirect bandgap nature of our NCs. Due to the detector‘s limitation, we did not observe a clear fluorescence behavior in other SnSe samples synthesized at 120−200 °C.

The size/shape dependent bandgaps in SnSe NCs were further simulated as shown in Fig. 3c. From the calculated total density of states of bulk SnSe [49], the bottom of the conduction band is around 4 eV below vacuum while the size of the valence band is around 5 eV. Since the excitation energies involved in this work are an order of magnitude smaller than these values, we can approximate excitons in SnSe NCs as quantum particles confined in an infinite potential well. In this framework, the NC bandgap is given by

+

n z
L z
2
where
E
g , b u l k
= 0.9 eV is the measured SnSe bulk bandgap [50], m0 is the electron rest mass, Lx , Ly and Lz are the NC sizes along the x, y and z axes, respectively, mx , my and mz are the electron-hole reduced effective masses, and nx , ny and nz are the mode numbers along the corresponding axis. Due to lack of knowledge of the effective masses in SnSe, for simplicity we will use the same value m* for mx , my and mz . In our particular case, where the two transverse lengths Lx and Ly of the NCs are identical and where only the first exciton mode nx = ny = nz = 1 is likely observed, Eq. (1) thus simplifies to
(2)

2

1
H 2
where we have defined Lx = Ly = L and Lz = H. Within this model, the normalized electron or hole wave function reads
(3)

2

where ξ stands for the x, y or z axis. The electron-hole reduced effective mass m* was estimated from the measurements of Ref. [50] for SnSe nanosheets, where L >> H. For values of H similar to those considered in this work (number of layers ≥ 15), one finds m*/m0 ≈ 0.06. For lower values of H, Eq. (2) overestimates, however, the bandgap measured in Ref. [50] due to effects not included in our simple model. The results of Eq. (2) using the NC sizes given in Table 1, compared to the corresponding measured bandgaps, are shown in Fig. 3c. One observes a good agreement, showing that Eq. (2) can parametrize the SnSe energy gap for the range of sizes considered in this work and the thickest nanosheets of Ref. [50].

To investigate the optoelectronic properties of SnSe NCs, as a proof-of-concept, we fabricated a PEC device for hydrogen evolution. We used a typical three-electrode configuration consisting of a QD-TiO2 photoanode, a platinum counter electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode, which is widely used for NCs based hydrogen evolution [5,21,42,47,48,51,52]. The electrolyte containing 0.25 M Na2S and 0.35 M Na2SO3 (pH∼13) was used as sacrificial hole scavenger. To compare the performance of the anode based on different size/shape of SnSe NCs, we used an anode whose thickness is similar to that of the TiO2 mesoporous film (8 μm) (Fig. S12a). The PEC device based on bare TiO2 exhibits a saturated current density of 0.3 mA/cm2 as the TiO2 can only absorb sunlight in the range 300−400 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 (a–d) and Table 1, upon one sun illumination (100 mW/cm2), the saturated photocurrent density in larger sized NCs (L/H: 21/12; 23/13 nm) is similar as that of TiO2 anode, indicating that there is no significant photoresponse from the NCs. In a big contrast, the saturated J increases with the decrease of NC size. When the L/H is 9 nm/7 nm, the saturated J goes up to ∼8.2 mA/cm2. To understand the size/shape dependent PEC behaviour, based on our simulation, we plotted the band energy levels of three size’s bandgap, and were presented in Fig. 5 b. Using the model discussed in Section 2.4, the electron and hole energy levels are given by (assuming the same effective mass 2m* for electrons and holes)
(5)
E e
=
E C
+
π 2
ℏ 2
4
m 0
m 0
m *
2
L 2
+
1
H 2
(6)
E h
=
E C
- E
g , b u l k
-
π 2
ℏ 2
4
m 0
m 0
m *
2
L 2
+
1
H 2
respectively, where EC is the lowest energy level of the conduction band (nearly the same as that of TiO2). The results of Eqs. (5) and (6) are illustrated in Fig. 5b relatively to EC . One clearly sees that the electron energy levels decreases and the hole energy level increase as the size of the NC decreases. The electron energy level Ee in large sized SnSe NCs is not favorable for charge transfer from NCs to TiO2, as well as the reduction energy level of water. This explains why there is no photoresponse in large-sized SnSe NCs. When the size decreases, the lowest energy level of the conduction band becomes higher than that of TiO2, due to strong quantum confinement effects, thus enabling the occurrence of efficient charge transfers. In SnSe NCs, the highest energy level of valence band is not favourable for water oxidation, so that there is no production of oxygen in our system and the photogenerated holes are transferred to hole scavengers (SO3 2−/S2−) (Fig. 5a). The smallest SnSe nanocrystals showed the best PEC performance results from to the two following reasons: (i) the smallest NCs can be more easily deposited into the mesoporous TiO2 film compared to larger size NC; (ii) the smallest NCs have larger offsets between the lowest conduction band and the energy levels of TiO2, which leads to faster charge transfer rates. The highest J value measured in this work (∼8.2 mA/cm2) is comparable to the ones reported from Cd or Pb based PEC systems (17.5 mA/cm2 of CdSe/CdSSe/CdS QDs and 22.1 mA/cm2 of Mn-PbS/CdS QDs [21,22]. To the best of our knowledge, this J is among the highest current densities ever obtained for earth-abundant near infrared colloidal NCs based PEC devices. As shown in Table S1, it is higher than SnSe nanoplatelets (∼7.4 mA/cm2) [47], Zn/In-doped SnS2 nanosheets (∼0.23 mA/cm2) [35], CuInSeS/ZnS QDs (∼5 mA/cm2) [48], and SnS2/CuInS2 nanosheets (∼4.5 mA/cm2) [26].

We further measured the stability of the anode (at 0.6 V vs RHE). The original current density drops by about 37 % after 7200 s (Fig. 5c). Metal chalogenide semiconductor NCs have been widely used as light absorbers for efficient hydrogen evolution, such as CdSe, CdSe/CdS, PbS/CdS, PbS-Mn/CdS, CdTe/CdS, CuInSe, and InP NCs [3,5,26,35,52–59] Nevertheless, it is a major challenge to obtain stable hydrogen evolution based on these materials due to the slow hole transfer rate, which requires to use electrolyte as hole scavenger [3,5,26,35,53,55–58]. As reported in the literature, for semiconductor NC based anode, during the PEC process, the strong corrosion may happen because of the high pH value of the electrolyte (pH = 13). In addition, the slower hole transport compared to that of photogenerated electrons may lead to hole accumulation [54]. Usually the size of SnSe is very small, the hole accumulation can induce the self-surface-oxidation of NCs, further leading to an increase in the number of surface defects/traps. Because of the above-mentioned two side effects, the as-prepared anode exhibits a quick decrease of current density at first 2-h cell operation, which is typical behavior for metal chalcogenide NCs based PEC devices [26,47]. On the other hand, between 2−5 h illumination, the current density is very stable, and there is no any significant decrease (Fig. 5c). This performance is comparable to that of the best previously reported photoanodes [3,5,26,35,52–59]. Based on our previous report, the Faraday efficiency is ∼95 % in a three-electrode system using Na2S/Na2SO3 as electrolyte [52]. We calculated the hydrogen evolution rate (see details in S.I., Fig. S13) using CdSe/CdS QDs as a reference [52]. The hydrogen generation rate is ∼52 mL cm−2 day−1, which is 24 % higher than the anode based on SnSe NPLs [47]. These results demonstrate that the SnSe NCs hold a great potential for solar-driven hydrogen evolution.

In summary, we demonstrated a facile cation exchange assisted method for synthesizing earth-abundant SnSe NCs. The SnSe NCs have a cuboid shape through the orientated growth path. These NCs exhibit typical indirect bandgaps ranging from 0.9 to 1.2 eV with tunable sizes/shapes. We have shown that a simple model of an exciton confined in the NC with a single reduced effective mass m*/m0 = 0.06 can satisfactorily reproduce the evolution of the measured bandgaps as a function of the SnSe NC sizes. The SnSe NCs-based photoanode exhibits a saturated J of ∼8.2 mA/cm2 at 1.0 V vs RHE. This value is ∼10 % higher than the previous record value for PEC devices using heavy metal-free colloidal NIR NCs under identical measurement conditions. In addition, PEC devices based on SnSe NCs exhibit an excellent photostability, in which the J of 5.2 mA/cm2 was maintained after 5 h illumination, leading to a hydrogen evolution rate of ∼52 mL cm−2 day−1.

The photo- and chemical- stability of SnSe NCs results in PEC devices with good stability. The cation exchange assisted strategy contributes a facile, scalable route to the field of earth-abundant NC synthesis. This approach may be used to produce SnS NCs and SnSe/SnS NCs by tuning the structure of the seeds. Our results indicate that earth-abundant SnSe NCs hold great potential as efficient light absorbers for optoelectronic devices, including PEC hydrogen evolution and solar cells.

H.Z. had the idea, designed and conducted the synthesis experiments. H.Z. supervised the work. C.W. and G.L. conducted the TEM measurement. C.W. conducted the XPS, EDS, SAED absorption and PL measurements, and analyzed the structure of NCs. H.Z. conducted XRD measurement and carried out the PEC device fabrication and characterizations. F.V. performed the theoretical calculations. H.Z. and C.W. wrote the first draft of the manuscript. F.R. provided major revision and editing of the manuscript. G.L, D.B. and G.H. participated in data analysis and corrections for the manuscript. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the manuscript.

Haiguang Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Supervision. Chao Wang: Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Guiju Liu: Data curation. David Barba: Writing - review & editing. François Vidal: Software, Validation. Guangting Han: Writing - review & editing. Federico Rosei: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H.G. Zhao acknowledges funding from the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2018MB001). F.R. acknowledges funding from the Natural Science and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC), the Canada Foundation for Innovation (CFI) for infrastructure support and its operating funds and the Fonds de recherche du Québec – Nature et technologies (FRQNT). F.R. thanks the Canada Research Chairs program for funding and partial salary support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2019.118526.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
